medications.indication_rationale: Strict nil-by-mouth is required pending emergency assessment given active bleeding and possible need for urgent endoscopy or surgery.
NSAIDs are a likely contributing cause of mucosal injury and must be stopped immediately; continuing use would worsen bleeding.
contraindications_in_this_case: Active suspected upper GI haemorrhage; NSAID likely contributory

medications.indication_rationale: Definitive resuscitation requiring emergency in-person administration; the telehealth role is to recognise the need and escalate, not to attempt any equivalent at home.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Lie flat with legs elevated and do not attempt to stand, sit up, or walk unassisted until paramedics arrive.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maximises cerebral perfusion and reduces fall risk given postural presyncope from hypovolaemia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once stabilised, work with the treating team and GP on reducing alcohol intake, which is a likely contributing cause of this bleed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces risk of recurrent mucosal injury and bleeding.

patient_education_points.point: Vomiting material that looks like coffee grounds, black tarry stools, and feeling faint when you stand up together suggest significant bleeding from your stomach or upper gut that needs emergency treatment right now.
Please call 000 for an ambulance immediately — this needs fluids, possibly a blood transfusion, and a camera test (endoscopy) in hospital to find and treat the source of bleeding.
Please stay lying flat with your legs up and do not try to stand, sit up, eat, or drink until the paramedics arrive.
If you feel any more vomiting coming on, try to lie on your side and spit rather than swallowing, so the team can see how much blood there is.
Stopping ibuprofen and cutting back on alcohol will be important once you're through this, as both can irritate the stomach lining and contribute to bleeding.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the emergency and gastroenterology team
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: repeat haemoglobin, review of endoscopy findings, Helicobacter pylori testing if peptic ulcer found, alcohol use support, medication review (avoid future NSAIDs)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any further vomiting of large volumes of blood, worsening dizziness, or loss of consciousness — call 000 immediately if not already en route.

must_include_items: Explicit statement that this is significant gastrointestinal bleeding requiring emergency treatment
Instruction to lie flat with legs elevated and not to stand unassisted
acceptable_alternatives: None — given presyncope and overt bleeding signs, ambulance dispatch is the only acceptable first action
errors_of_omission: No mention this could be serious/life-threatening bleeding
errors_of_commission: Recommending the patient eat, drink, or attempt to stand/walk before assessment
Recommending a routine GP appointment instead of emergency care
Spending extended time exploring the alcohol history before escalating
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) immediate 000 call AND (2) explicit serious GI bleeding framing is an automatic FAIL. This is a T5 case with signs of early hypovolaemic shock.
